questionText: The marks, $X$, scored by candidates in an examination are modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, 12^2)$, where the mean $\mu$ is unknown. It is known that $90\%$ of candidates score less than $75$ marks. Find $\mu$, and hence find the proportion of candidates who score more than $60$ marks. Give the proportion to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution with unknown mean
steps.explanation: The standard deviation is known to be $12$ but the mean is not. The percentile information will let us pin down $\mu$, after which we can answer the follow-up.
'Ninety percent score less than $75$' means the value $75$ is the $90$th percentile, so the area below it is $0.90$.

steps.description: Find the z-value for the 90th percentile
steps.explanation: The standardised value with $90\%$ of the area beneath it is $1.282$; whatever the mean is, $75$ must standardise to this $z$.
Standardising $75$ gives $\frac{75-\mu}{\sigma}$, and this must equal $1.282$, giving one equation in the single unknown $\mu$.
Multiplying by $\sigma = 12$ and moving terms across isolates the mean.
Evaluating gives the mean mark of the examination.
This is the average mark; note it is sensibly below $75$, since $75$ sits up in the top $10\%$.
With the mean now known, the proportion scoring more than $60$ is $P(X > 60)$.
We convert $60$ into a $z$-score; it is only just above the mean, so the $z$-value is small and positive.
The probability of exceeding $60$ is one minus the cumulative area below it.
Just above the mean the cumulative value is a little over one half.
Subtracting from one gives the proportion of candidates scoring above $60$.
So about $48.7\%$ of candidates score more than $60$ marks.

steps.workingLatex: 60 \text{ is just above } \mu = 59.62 \Rightarrow P(X > 60) \text{ just below } 0.5
steps.explanation: Because $60$ is only fractionally above the mean, we expect just under half of candidates to beat it; $0.487$ is indeed a little below $0.5$, confirming the result.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $\mu \approx 59.6$ marks, and the proportion scoring more than $60$ is $P(X > 60) \approx 0.487$.
